video_title: DeFi Hacks Happening Every Day; Institutions Are Still Coming
gist: A Defiant panel with Kraken (John Settler), Veda (Sun Raghupathi) and Centrifuge/Centura (Anthony D. Martino) argues the recent ~$1.1B/12mo of DeFi losses are mostly key-management/supply-chain failures rather than smart-contract bugs, so risk is migrating from the contract layer to the operational layer. They make the case that pooled lending (Aave) survives because of balance-sheet backstops, that vault 'risk curation' needs disclosure and insurance, and that despite gloomy crypto price action, enterprise/fintech adoption of yield-bearing DeFi is accelerating, not slowing.

themes.theme: Hack post-mortem: supply-chain vs smart-contract risk

insights.claim: The recent wave of DeFi losses (~$630M in April, ~$1.1B over the trailing 12 months) is dominated by supply-chain/key-compromise attacks (e.g., Bybit) rather than smart-contract vulnerabilities, where an individual insider is phished via a malicious PDF rather than the contract code being broken.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Distinguishing operational from contract risk reframes where due diligence and capital should go — audit-clean protocols can still be drained via human/key failure, so investors should price operational controls (multisig, time-locks) not just audit status.
insights.quote: a number of the attacks, the bybit one... a lot of these have been supply chain attacks versus actually smart contract vulnerabilities. I think that's a really important distinction.

insights.claim: OpenZeppelin co-founder Manuel Aroz publicly recommended exiting DeFi entirely, but the firm OpenZeppelin distanced itself from its former founder's claim — a notable signal because OpenZeppelin audited nearly all of DeFi's code.
insights.speaker: Cam Russo (host) / John Settler
insights.so_what: A capitulation call from a top auditor's co-founder is a sentiment marker near a cycle low; the firm's pushback suggests the bearish take is contested, not consensus.
insights.quote: the co-founder of Open Zeppelin, Manuel Ara, said that he recommends getting out of DeFi entirely. This is the guy who co-founded the auditing firm that's reviewed almost all of DeFi code.

insights.claim: The LayerZero/Kelp DAO incident — where a counterfeit rsETH was created via a LayerZero DVN setup, posted as collateral, and real ETH was drained from Aave — is the clearest recent example of an actual smart-contract-layer exploit, but DeFi participants coordinated to make users whole.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Cross-protocol DVN/oracle trust assumptions are a live attack surface; the fact that recovery hinged on Aave's backstop (not code) is the key tell for where systemic safety actually resides.
insights.quote: this kind of counterfeit RSE was created, posted as collateral, and then real we was stolen from the A protocol

insights.claim: Frontier AI models (referencing an 'Mythos'/Anthropic-class model said to be finding vulnerabilities) are likely being used offensively for exploit discovery, and North Korea/Lazarus is suspected of deploying a similar advanced model to find code vulnerabilities, since crypto theft is among North Korea's largest economic imports.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: AI-accelerated offense raises the baseline exploit rate and shortens the time-to-attack on weak protocols — a structural tax on under-audited, under-secured DeFi that favors scale incumbents.
insights.quote: Lazarus North Korea is probably using something similar to a mythos model... stealing money from crypto investors is seems to be one of the biggest imports of the North Korean economy

insights.claim: Host's contrarian pushback: framing an exploit as 'only a supply-chain or private-key issue, not a smart-contract hack' does not reduce investor risk, because a safe contract surrounded by unsafe off-chain configuration still loses user funds.
insights.speaker: Cam Russo (host)
insights.so_what: Investors should treat the 'it wasn't the contract' defense as a red flag, not reassurance; total system risk = contract risk + operational/key risk, and the latter is now the bigger driver.
insights.quote: it's like, okay, great, your smart contract is safe, but all the rest isn't. So... I'm not very reassured when I hear those types of arguments

themes.theme: Risk migrates to the operational layer

insights.claim: DeFi risk is structurally moving from the smart-contract layer to the operational layer, so the industry's next priority must be establishing standards (akin to SOC 2 in cybersecurity) for which roles and keys control user funds, with full transparency to depositors.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: Protocols that publish role/key configurations and operational-security disclosures will earn a trust premium; this becomes a differentiator and a screen for allocators.
insights.quote: Risk is now moving from the smart contract layer into the operational layer. And that is not trivial

insights.claim: Many of the past year's 'exploits' stem from a highly-permissioned role being held by an externally-owned account (EOA) rather than a multisig gated by a time-lock — a basic, solvable design failure rather than a code break.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: A concrete, checkable due-diligence test: verify no fund-bearing role is controlled by a bare EOA; presence of EOA-controlled admin keys is a direct disqualifier.
insights.quote: a highly permissioned role being granted to a very low security holder like an EOA as opposed to something like a multisig gated by a time lock

insights.claim: Vaults have a smaller attack surface than lending protocols or DEXes because their only public-facing functions are deposit and withdraw; the complexity lives in internal, permissioned roles (adding markets, setting withdrawal cooldowns), making role configuration the primary security variable.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: When underwriting a vault, scrutinize internal role permissions and guardrails rather than public function complexity — the risk is concentrated in admin design, not user-facing surface.
insights.quote: the only public facing functions are deposit and withdraw because that's all users need to do

insights.claim: Risk management must be continuous, not a point-in-time audit; Centura compares a one-time audit to putting a single lock on a house versus deploying cameras, sensors and a dog, arguing dynamic markets make audit-only security inadequate.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Allocators should favor protocols/curators with live monitoring over those relying on a stale audit; 'audited' is necessary but not sufficient.
insights.quote: an audit is done as a moment in time... relying on a point in time is yeah obviously not the right solution

insights.claim: Centura runs a proprietary 'Risk Radar' that flags real-time anomalies (bad debt approaching a threshold on a money market, large inflows/outflows, oracle price divergences) and embeds on-chain risk triggers in deployment contracts that auto-unwind positions — e.g., if a stablecoin drops below 0.9997 the contract returns funds to the user's wallet.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Automated depeg-triggered unwinds are a concrete mitigation investors can demand; the specific 0.9997 threshold shows how tight the guardrails can be set for stable-collateral strategies.
insights.quote: if a stable coin drops below 9997, the smart contract has authorization to unwind the position and put it back in the user's wallet

insights.claim: Centura screened out rsETH before the Kelp exploit — it was never approved or included in any Kraken or Centura vault — illustrating that deep per-asset due diligence (Centura claims a ~6-month process) can pre-empt key-management/asset-level failures.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Asset-selection discipline is a measurable edge; a curator that rejected the asset that later blew up is a track-record signal worth weighting.
insights.quote: RS was never approved and was never part of any any Kraken vault or Centur vault

themes.theme: Composability: from cure-all to managed liability

insights.claim: Contagion and composability are the newly-salient risks: the Kelp incident woke participants to the fact that an exposure in one protocol can damage an unrelated protocol, so 'money Legos' must be treated as a risk channel, not just a feature.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Map second-order exposure: a vault's risk includes every protocol its collateral touches, so 'I'm not using protocol X' is no protection if your collateral is connected to X.
insights.quote: the concept of contagion where something over here can actually impact something over there

insights.claim: Reflexive composability is now seen as a mistake for complex assets: making a vault receipt token usable as collateral elsewhere is 'almost never worth it from a risk perspective'; composability should be reserved for simple assets like staking tokens and stablecoins.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: A maturation thesis that contradicts the early-DeFi 'infinite composability' dogma; vault tokens deliberately kept non-composable are lower-risk, and the long-tail-lending-everything thesis is fading.
insights.quote: not every asset needs to be collateral in a lending protocol not every asset needs to have liquidity on dexes

insights.claim: Risk isolation is the key structural defense, delivered two ways: protocol-level isolated markets (pioneered by Morpho, extended by Aave's v4 instance) and the vault itself acting as a curated isolation layer where a risk manager chooses which collateral types are included.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Favor isolated-market designs (Morpho/Aave v4) over shared-pool exposure for raw collateral plays; isolation limits blast radius when a single asset fails.
insights.quote: Morpho pioneered this with their isolated markets and A has extended it with their A v4 instance

themes.theme: Future of on-chain lending: pooled vs isolated

insights.claim: Isolated lending (Morpho, Euler) has been vindicated as the venue for self-directed, raw-exposure positions, but it is unproven whether isolated lending can charge meaningful fees at scale — the economics of the primitive are still untested.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: Isolated-lending tokens/protocols carry monetization uncertainty; the primitive is valuable but its fee-capture (hence token value accrual) is the open question to watch.
insights.quote: we don't know whether isolated lending can charge fees. We just haven't seen it at scale

insights.claim: Aave is the only protocol that could absorb the Kelp-style exploit without users taking losses because its strong balance sheet/treasury let it bring parties together and backstop deposits — making pooled lending a 'scale business' that an under-capitalized protocol cannot replicate.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: Balance-sheet strength is a moat: Aave's ability to make users whole gives it pricing power and a healthy treasury, a flywheel that favors the incumbent and is a reason to weight AAVE over smaller pooled lenders.
insights.quote: there is only one protocol on the planet that could have come back from this without users taking losses and that's a

insights.claim: Pooled lending is currently 'the only thing that makes money' in on-chain lending because users pay for trusted DAO-brand risk management, giving Aave pricing power; both pooled and isolated models will coexist rather than one replacing the other.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: Revenue/fee durability currently sits with brand-backed pooled lenders; a barbell of pooled-incumbent (fees + backstop) plus isolated-primitive (flexibility) is the likely market structure.
insights.quote: poolled lending is the only thing that makes money right now

themes.theme: Vault business model, curation and incentives

insights.claim: Vault curators on Morpho face a perverse incentive: whoever first adds the highest-yielding asset attracts the most stablecoin deposits, gets the most looping and the most fees — pushing curators to onboard unworthy assets to chase TVL.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Yield-chasing curator incentives are a systemic vault risk; treat unusually high vault yields as a signal of riskier collateral admission, not curator skill.
insights.quote: the p the first person that adds a 10% asset sees all the stable coins come to their vault. It gets looped. They make the most amount of fees

insights.claim: Centura deliberately stayed out of the vault market until October last year and rejects assets like Usual, Stream and Resolve that launched within a week, because its own asset due-diligence takes roughly six months — explicitly trading yield/TVL growth for risk discipline.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Names Usual/Stream/Resolve as assets a disciplined curator rejected — a watchlist of higher-risk yield sources; the 6-month vs 1-week onboarding gap quantifies the diligence spectrum.
insights.quote: things like usual or stream or resolve more recently. There's this demand to bring assets that are just not worthy to the market

insights.claim: There is a meaningful distinction between a 'vault curator' (a name on a Morpho vault) and 'risk curation' — the Kraken/Veda/Centura partnership frames itself as the latter, a more complex risk-managed approach than simply spinning up a Bitcoin-vs-stablecoin vault.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Branding/positioning move that signals a tiering of vault providers; allocators should distinguish thin 'curators' from full risk-curation stacks when comparing vault TVL and yields.
insights.quote: the name sitting on a morpho vault is a curator. What the three of us do together is a risk curation. It's a much different level of risk management

insights.claim: Centura's stated mandate as a Morpho vault curator is to protect stablecoin depositors, not leveraged speculators — calibrating asset-price risk against LTVs and publishing prospectus-like disclosures (e.g., for its vault using Figure's prime product) rather than claiming zero risk.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Know whom a vault is designed to protect: stablecoin-depositor-protective vaults are structurally safer for conservative capital than yield-max vaults that implicitly favor leveraged users.
insights.quote: we are there to protect the stable coin depositors in those falls

insights.claim: The core structural advantage over TradFi managers is that vaults are non-custodial with cryptographic guardrails: a curator like Centura cannot swap depositor Bitcoin to Doge or take hidden leverage, and depositors can see live positions and withdraw at will — unlike opaque centralized earn products (BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager) that blew up.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: The verifiability + withdrawal-rights feature set is the genuine edge of on-chain vaults over the last cycle's CeFi yield products; it is the underwriting basis for trusting these structures with retail capital.
insights.quote: that vault cannot take your Bitcoin and swap it to Doge and just do crazy trading with it

insights.claim: Independent risk ratings for vaults are coming, potentially from credible traditional risk raters interested in coming on-chain to assess vaults — an anticipated layer of institutional trust infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: Watch for TradFi rating agencies entering on-chain vault assessment; their arrival would be a catalyst for larger institutional allocations and a screen investors can use.
insights.quote: There will be independent risk raers including potentially some of the most credible traditional institutions

insights.claim: Kraken's new Bitcoin Vault routes user BTC through a three-party stack — Kraken (front-end/distribution, ~30M users) issues a KBTC wrapper (redeemable 1:1 for Bitcoin at Kraken), Veda issues a receipt token and routes capital, and Centura deploys into protocols (Morpho, Aave) and strategies.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken) / Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Defines the distribution-to-yield pipeline (CEX front-end → wrapper → vault → lending) that channels mainstream BTC into DeFi yield — a template likely to be replicated across exchanges.
insights.quote: Kraken is the the front end... 30 million users... Those funds flow into VETA and VETA gives them a receipt token for their share of the pool and then those funds come to the Centur side

insights.claim: The Bitcoin yield mechanism is a borrow/lend carry: KBTC is posted as collateral in a borrow-lend protocol, stablecoins are borrowed against it and deployed to farm yield, and the spread between farm yield and borrow cost (converted back to BTC) is the return to depositors.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Understand the return source: this is a levered basis/carry trade whose yield compresses if borrow costs rise or farm yields fall — not native Bitcoin yield, and exposed to depeg and rate risk.
insights.quote: the Bitcoin or the KBDC is being deposited as collateral into a borrow lend protocol. Stable coins are being borrowed against it. Those stable coins are then being deployed to farm yield and then that spread... is the return

insights.claim: The Kraken Bitcoin Vault reached ~$60M TVL within one day of its launch and grew faster than Kraken's prior USDC vault launch, with institutional inbound (custody/Prime access requests) trailing retail demand.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Strong day-one BTC-yield demand quantifies the appetite for Bitcoin yield ('the white whale'); faster ramp than the USDC vault suggests BTC-yield is the higher-demand product category.
insights.quote: we launched this yesterday and we're already at like $60 million in TVL which is pretty crazy. Like it's really exciting and grew a lot faster even than our prior USDC vaults launch

insights.claim: Earlier Bitcoin-yield attempts failed because they used opaque TradFi-style lending desks (BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager) that took excessive risk and gave users no way to underwrite it; the on-chain, transparent, KBTC-wrapped approach is positioned as the corrected version.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Frames the prior CeFi-yield blowups as a solvable transparency problem, supporting the thesis that on-chain BTC yield can attract the capital that fled after 2022.
insights.quote: they pursued it in a very wrong way. They went with these like, you know, opaque Tradfy style BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager type of lending desks that were taking on way too much risk and blew up

insights.claim: Centura is building 'Firelight,' a DeFi insurance product that converts hard-to-understand DeFi risk into over-collateralization risk that users do understand — depositors accept slightly lower yield in exchange for a collateral buffer that fully covers or caps losses, which the panel expects to become table stakes for the next institutional wave.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: DeFi has lacked an FDIC analog; an over-collateralized insurance layer that becomes 'table stakes' could be the unlock for the next large institutional wave — a product category to watch.
insights.quote: we're building a product called Firelight, which is DeFi insurance... convert the DeFi risk that people don't understand into overcolateralization risk

insights.claim: The next ~$150B into DeFi will not be DeFi-native — it will arrive via enterprise channels (remittance companies, neobanks, payments firms) that move the traditional model of earning a fraction of T-bills on fiat toward stablecoins-in-a-wallet earning DeFi yield.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: The marginal DeFi buyer is now enterprises abstracting DeFi for retail; infrastructure that handles abstraction, compliance and distribution captures this $150B, not consumer dApps.
insights.quote: the next 150 billion that's coming into DeFi is not DeFi native. It is coming through the channels that John opened up

insights.claim: Despite the April hacks, Centura closed three enterprise-payments earn deals in the following five weeks (all but one proceeded), and contagion fears did not produce the expected pullback — bank and asset-manager conversations heated up rather than slowed.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Hard evidence that enterprise demand is rate-insensitive to DeFi exploit headlines — a counter-signal to the bearish 'institutions will flee' narrative.
insights.quote: since April... we actually landed three deals in the last five weeks... we've actually seen all but one of those deals actually go through

insights.claim: Banks view vaults as a way to move lending off balance sheet (a blockchain version of an SPV) with capital and client benefits, while asset managers want leverage/looping and are bringing new real-world-asset-backed products (RBAs) to be deployed into vault structures.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: Identifies two distinct institutional demand drivers — off-balance-sheet lending (banks) and levered RWA distribution (asset managers) — that define which vault/RWA infrastructure benefits.
insights.quote: banks see vaults as a way to basically move their lending business off balance sheet... it's kind of like a electronic or a blockchain version of an SPV

insights.claim: Enterprises proceed despite DeFi risk because the competitive stakes are existential: payments providers retain very little of their high volume on-platform, but offering on-chain yield lets them capture, retain and monetize far more assets — so being even six months behind a competitor is a substantial disadvantage.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: Explains the durability of enterprise demand through a unit-economics lens — yield is a customer-retention weapon, so adoption is driven by competitive necessity, not risk appetite.
insights.quote: if you don't do it, and even if you're six months behind and your competitor does it, you're at a substantial disadvantage

insights.claim: Institutional vault demand differs from retail on three axes: institutions are slower and do deeper diligence, require compliance/KYC restrictions on vault participants, and expect thinner institutional-scale pricing versus richer retail pricing — but otherwise want the same yield products.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: Productizing for institutions means adding KYC-gated, compliance-aware vaults at thinner spreads; the yield design itself need not change, lowering the cost to serve institutional capital.
insights.quote: they sometimes have slightly different prompt requirements such as compliance, KYC... institutional scale pricing is usually a bit thinner

themes.theme: Macro/regulatory tailwinds and the fundamentals-vs-price divergence

insights.claim: US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's prediction of ~$3 trillion in stablecoins by 2030 — framed as stabilizing foreign Treasury ownership and exporting dollar hegemony through dollar-dominated trade — was a marquee catalyst that gave institutions like JP Morgan cover to treat DeFi/stablecoins as inevitable.
insights.speaker: Anthony D. Martino (Centura)
insights.so_what: A top-down policy endorsement reframes stablecoins as a strategic dollar tool, de-risking institutional participation — a structural tailwind for stablecoin issuers and dollar-denominated DeFi yield.
insights.quote: Scott best the treasury secretary of the United States basically put out a prediction of three trillion of stable coins by 2030

insights.claim: Four fundamental forces are converging in DeFi's favor — surging stablecoin adoption/market cap, a friendlier US regulatory climate (Genius Act passed, Clarity Act expected to pass), expanding borrow-lend/vault financing activity, and real-world assets coming on-chain — each described as 'custom-built' for institutions.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: A consolidated bull framework for allocators: legislative clarity plus stablecoin and RWA growth plus vault financing are mutually reinforcing institutional on-ramps — the multi-year DeFi-adoption thesis.
insights.quote: I'm talking about Genius Act. I'm talking about Clarity Act which looks like it's going to pass... UA's real world assets in all forms

insights.claim: There is now a near-complete decoupling between DeFi price action (described as 'garbage,' echoing a 2022-style four-year-cycle decline) and fundamentals (stablecoin usage, regulation, financing, RWA all improving) — and institutions are entering precisely because they focus on fundamentals, not token prices.
insights.speaker: John Settler (Kraken)
insights.so_what: The price-fundamentals gap is the central investable tension: bearish charts may mask the strongest fundamental backdrop ever, arguing for accumulation by fundamentals-driven capital despite weak price.
insights.quote: there is just this complete separation now between price action and fundamentals. And on one hand, price action just looks garbage... but in the fundamentals world, things have never been better

insights.claim: The DeFi customer base has structurally shifted from speculative 'degens' (memecoins, NFTs, game-theory yield schemes) to enterprises — capital, funding and developer focus have moved away from servicing the marginal degen, making it hard to launch a new project that can't sell to enterprises or prove code at billion-dollar scale.
insights.speaker: Sun Raghupathi (Veda)
insights.so_what: A 'feast or famine' regime favoring battle-tested, enterprise-ready protocols over experimental new entrants — relevant for token selection (incumbents win) and for the muted on-chain speculative activity.
insights.quote: The natural customer of DeFi is no longer dens... nobody cares about memecoins anymore
